title: Uniqueness and Convexity in the Calculus of Variations
speaker: Bernd Kirchheim
abstract:
In the Calculus of Variations, convexity plays a seemingly irreplaceable role.
For vectorial problems, however, for good reasons a whole zoo of its generalizations was introduced. It ranges  from notions based on very classical
observation over explicite, but only partially understood,
characterizations to very practical  conditions.
Recently a new convexity concept emerged, in differnt research projects for
different reasons. We are interested in it, since it describes stabilty of
uniqueness of minimizers under a huge class of pertubations.
We'll discuss differences, and particular
agreement with other convexity notions for vectorial problems.

proof_stages.label: Autonomous integral functional and classical variations
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up autonomous variational integrals ∫Ω f(∇u) and recalls the Euler–Lagrange equation and Legendre–Hadamard second-variation condition as the classical necessary conditions for minimizers.

proof_stages.label: Morrey quasiconvexity and rank-one convexity
proof_stages.summary: Morrey’s criterion is presented: weak lower semicontinuity of the integral is equivalent to quasiconvexity, while high-frequency rank-one tests give the weaker necessary rank-one convexity condition.

proof_stages.label: Null Lagrangians, polyconvexity, and the convexity hierarchy
proof_stages.summary: The determinant and the minors are used to explain null Lagrangians and polyconvexity, motivating the hierarchy polyconvex ⇒ quasiconvex ⇒ rank-one convex and the need for such notions in nonconvex multiwell energies.

proof_stages.label: Uniqueness under linear perturbations as an abstract convexity principle
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces perturbed energies E(u) minus a linear force and recalls an abstract functional-analytic theorem saying that convex nonempty minimizer sets for a norm-dense convex class of perturbations force the functional itself to be convex.

proof_stages.label: Functional convexity and localization for integral energies
proof_stages.summary: Functional convexity is defined as convexity of the integral energy on affine Sobolev classes g+W^{1,∞}_0(Ω), and a rescaling/interpolation argument shows that it is independent of the particular domain and boundary datum.

proof_stages.label: Quasiconvexity and quasimonotone slope as necessary consequences
proof_stages.summary: By testing functional convexity along t↦A+t∇u and using the vanishing first variation at t=0, the speaker derives quasiconvexity and the stronger derivative condition called a quasimonotone slope.

proof_stages.label: Proper-minor nonlinearities obstruct functional convexity
proof_stages.summary: A Hamburger-style laminate construction, exemplified by det^2, shows that nonlinear convex dependence on higher minors destroys functional convexity, leading to a proposition that a function φ of proper minors must be affine unless compensated by sufficient convexity in the matrix entries.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity theorems and remaining high-growth regimes
proof_stages.summary: The main rigidity results are stated: under subquadratic growth functional convexity forces ordinary convexity, while related sublinear-second-derivative or isotropic hypotheses give convexity up to null-Lagrangian terms, leaving higher-growth and two-homogeneous cases as open terrain.

examples.name: Determinant as a null Lagrangian
examples.note: The determinant is used to show that quasiconvexity need not imply ordinary convexity, and this visibly motivates the move to null Lagrangians and then polyconvex dependence on minors.
examples.engagement_reason: The determinant is cited as the famous null Lagrangian and its boundary-integral invariance is stated, but not derived or computed.

passes.reason: The determinant is variationally null, with boundary-only integral, and is used to show quasiconvexity need not imply convexity.
As a variationally null boundary term with high determinant symmetry, it is invoked as the famous quasiconvex-but-not-convex example.
The determinant is a highly invariant null Lagrangian whose variational integral is boundary-only, used to show quasiconvexity need not imply convexity.
The determinant is the top null Lagrangian/volume form, saturating the quasiconvex inequality, and is used to show quasiconvexity need not imply convexity.
The determinant is a variationally null Lagrangian used explicitly to show quasiconvexity need not imply convexity.
the determinant is a null Lagrangian whose integral depends only on boundary data, and it is explicitly used to show quasiconvexity need not imply convexity
Used to refute quasiconvexity implying convexity; the determinant is a boundary-only null Lagrangian giving equality in the quasiconvex inequality.
used explicitly to show quasiconvexity need not imply convexity, and as a null Lagrangian its integral is boundary-determined and variationally degenerate
Used as the standard null-Lagrangian counterexample to quasiconvex implying convex, with variationally degenerate boundary-only integral behavior.
Used explicitly to show Morrey-quasiconvexity need not imply convexity, while the determinant is a null Lagrangian giving only a boundary/equality contribution.
It is explicitly used to refute “quasiconvex implies convex,” and as a null Lagrangian the determinant is a highly symmetric variationally-null boundary term saturating the quasiconvex inequality.
Used as the standard null-Lagrangian counterexample to “quasiconvex implies convex,” and it is variationally degenerate since its fixed-boundary integral is a boundary quantity.

examples.name: Ball-type polyconvex elasticity energy
examples.note: The energy h(det X)+|X|^2, with h singular as det X approaches zero, illustrates why polyconvexity is useful in nonlinear elasticity despite failure of ordinary convexity.
examples.engagement_reason: The Ball-type energy h(det X)+|X|^2 is given as an example of a polyconvex elasticity energy, with no concrete calculation on it.

passes.reason: This is a standard Ball polyconvex elasticity ansatz used positively to illustrate polyconvexity.
The Ball-type integrand is a standard isotropic polyconvex elasticity prototype used as a positive application rather than to refute a claim.
The Ball-type energy |X|^2+h(det X) is a rotationally invariant polyconvex elasticity model used as a positive motivating example.
Ball’s |X|^2+h(det X)-type elasticity energy is a rotationally invariant polyconvex model used as a positive motivating example.
Ball's norm-plus-determinant-barrier energy is an isotropic polyconvex model presented as a standard positive example from elasticity.
Ball's energy such as |X|^2+h(det X) is a standard rotationally invariant polyconvex elasticity model used as a positive motivating example
Presented as a positive Ball-style polyconvex elasticity model, with rotationally invariant norm and determinant dependence.
presented as a standard positive model of Ball-style polyconvex elasticity energy, not as a refutation
Offered as a standard positive polyconvex elasticity model of Ball type rather than to refute a claim.
Presented as a positive standard Ball polyconvex elasticity model, with isotropic dependence on |X| and det X.
It is presented as Ball’s standard isotropic polyconvex elasticity energy such as |X|^2+h(det X), motivating applications rather than defeating a claim.
Ball’s h(det X)+|X|²-type energy is used as a positive motivating example of polyconvex elasticity, with isotropic rotation symmetry.

examples.name: Three-well shape-memory martensite model
examples.note: The distance of ∇u to SO(3) times three diagonal variants models a cubic-to-rectangular phase transition and illustrates why multiwell nonconvex energies require quasiconvex relaxation rather than convexity.
examples.engagement_reason: The three wells are written out, related to the lambda-dependent variants, and the speaker reasons through compatibility, rank-one connections, mixing, and boundary effects.

passes.reason: The SO(3)-related three martensitic wells form a symmetric nonconvex zero set used to show ordinary convexity is too strong.
The symmetrically permuted three-well martensite set is used to show that ordinary convexity is untenable for multiwell shape-memory energies.
The three SO(3)-related martensitic wells have cubic-variant symmetry and are invoked to show ordinary convexity cannot model such multiwell energies.
The three martensitic wells are symmetry-related SO(3)-orbits and are used to show natural multiwell energies cannot be ordinarily convex.
The three symmetry-related martensitic wells are used to show that realistic multiwell energies cannot be ordinarily convex.
the three symmetry-related martensitic wells give a physically natural energy whose zero set is nonconvex, obstructing ordinary convexity
Used to show natural martensite multiwell energies need not be convex; the three equivalent wells are related by crystal/rotational symmetries.
used to show that physically natural multiwell martensite energies have nonconvex zero sets, obstructing ordinary convexity assumptions
The three symmetry-related martensitic wells are used to show ordinary convexity cannot capture natural multiwell energies.
Used to show physically relevant multiwell energies have nonconvex zero sets and therefore cannot be ordinary convex.
The three equivalent SO(3)-wells from permuted martensitic stretches have cubic symmetry and are used to show ordinary convexity is too restrictive for such materials.
The three SO(3)-orbits of permutation-related martensitic stretches are used to show ordinary convexity is impossible for such multiwell energies.

examples.name: Distance-squared to a nonconvex set of conformal matrices
examples.note: In the Q&A, the speaker describes taking the squared distance to a small nonconvex set in the conformal matrices and then its quasiconvex envelope as a source of interesting nonconvex functionally convex candidates.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the distance-square and quasiconvexification/relaxation construction and checks structurally that conformal matrices have no rank-one connections, so the zero set is not pulled down.

passes.reason: The rank-one-free nonconvex conformal zero set is offered as a nonconvex non-determinant-type example against convex-only expectations.
The nonconvex conformal-well distance energy is offered as a nonconvex, non-determinant-type example, not as a sporadic or extremal object.
The squared distance to a nonconvex conformal zero set is used to exhibit a nonconvex/non-determinant-type example, with no sporadic or limiting structure specified.
The squared distance to a nonconvex conformal well set is offered as a nonconvex relaxation example rather than a sporadic or boundary object.
The distance-squared to a nonconvex conformal set is offered as a constructed nonconvex relaxation example rather than as a direct refutation of a stated implication.
the squared distance to a nonconvex conformal multiwell set is offered as a nonconvex rank-one-free example rather than as a formal disproof of a stated claim
Used as a nonconvex, non-determinant-type conformal-well example; the particular nonconvex subset is not specified as extremal, degenerate, symmetric, or sporadic.
offered to produce a nonconvex, non-determinant-type example from conformal wells
Presented as an interesting rank-one-free conformal-well construction, not as a direct disproof of a stated claim.
Offered as a nonconvex, non-determinant-type distance energy whose conformal wells obstruct ordinary convexity.
It is offered as a nonconvex, non-determinant-type example coming from the highly regular conformal-matrix setting, so it challenges overly convex/determinant-type expectations.
The distance-square construction is invoked to exhibit a nonconvex, non-determinant-type relaxed example rather than a convex one.

examples.name: The family |X|^4+k det X
examples.note: This family illustrates that quasimonotone-slope-type conditions and full functional convexity can have different parameter ranges, with functional convexity coinciding with ordinary convexity in the cited result.
examples.engagement_reason: The family |X|^4+k det X is recalled with known parameter ranges for quasimonotonicity and functional convexity, but those facts are only cited, not derived.

passes.reason: The family has parameters that are quasimonotone but not functionally convex, separating the two notions.
This rotationally invariant polynomial family is used, for the discussed k-ranges, to separate quasimonotonicity from functional convexity.
The SO(2)-invariant polynomial family is used in the range 2<k≤8 as quasi-monotone but not functionally convex.
The isotropic one-parameter family |X|^4+k det X is used, for suitable k, to separate quasimonotonicity from functional convexity.
The polynomial family is used through parameter ranges where quasimonotonicity holds but functional convexity fails, separating the notions.
